citations.proposition: Seizure reasonableness is judged under an objective standard from the perspective of a reasonable officer on the scene; the analysis weighs, among other factors, 'the severity of the crime at issue.' Anchors Golden Rule #2 (seriousness of the crime/circumstance bears on reasonableness).

Reasonableness is judged on 'the facts available to the officer at the moment of the seizure or the search' that would 'warrant a man of reasonable caution in the belief' the action was appropriate; also the source of the reasonable-suspicion tier. Anchors Golden Rule #1 (articulable facts) and the burden-of-proof ladder.

VERIFIED VERBATIM: 'In dealing with probable cause, however, as the very name implies, we deal with probabilities. These are not technical; they are the factual and practical considerations of everyday life on which reasonable and prudent men, not legal technicians, act.' Anchors Golden Rule #3 (probabilities, not possibilities).

VERIFIED VERBATIM: 'probable cause is a fluid concept - turning on the assessment of probabilities in particular factual contexts - not readily, or even usefully, reduced to a neat set of legal rules.' Adopts the totality-of-the-circumstances test. Anchors Golden Rule #3.

VERIFIED VERBATIM: a protective sweep requires 'articulable facts which, taken together with the rational inferences from those facts, would warrant a reasonably prudent officer in believing that the area to be swept harbors an individual posing a danger to those on the arrest scene' (494 U.S. at 334); it is 'decidedly not "automati[c]"' (494 U.S. at 336); scope limited to a cursory inspection of spaces where a person may be found, lasting no longer than needed to dispel the danger (494 U.S. at 335-36). Controlling FEDERAL rule for protective sweeps.

citations.case: Gaetjens v. Winnebago County (Gaetjens v. City of Loves Park)
citations.bluebook: Gaetjens v. Winnebago County, 4 F.4th 487 (7th Cir. 2021)
citations.court: U.S. Court of Appeals for the Seventh Circuit
citations.proposition: VERIFIED: emergency-aid exigency - government officials may enter a home without a warrant 'to render assistance or prevent harm to persons or property within'; entry lawful where the officer had 'an objectively reasonable basis for believing that [the occupant] was experiencing a medical emergency that required immediate action.' Also requires a nexus between the emergency and the place entered, and scope limited to the emergency. Illustrates Golden Rule #2 (seriousness/urgency widens reasonableness). NOTE: caption is 'Sally Gaetjens v. Winnebago County, Illinois' (Loves Park is in Winnebago County); the note's 'City of Loves Park' is the home's location, not the case caption. Civil sec. 1983/Monell posture but applies the Fourth Amendment exigency standard. read_document opinion id = 4703206 (cluster id = 4899427).

non_case_items.note: Instructor maxim, NOT a legal rule. Articulate at least five factors for any action - a habit to build articulation, not a five-factor Fourth Amendment requirement. Stated plainly on this page; no case verifies it because it is a heuristic. Lives on this page per the brief.
Instructor articulation drill (form of expression), not a legal standard.

Instructor-development maxims, not citation-verifiable. Captured verbatim in substance and stated plainly; each maxim's legal CORE is anchored to verified federal authority (Terry/Graham/Brinegar/Gates/Buie).

Pedagogical hierarchy: mere suspicion -> reasonable suspicion -> probable cause -> preponderance -> clear and convincing -> beyond a reasonable doubt. The reasonable-suspicion and probable-cause rungs are anchored to Terry and Gates/Brinegar; the civil/criminal burdens (preponderance, clear & convincing, BRD) are standard evidentiary tiers, not Fourth Amendment holdings.

corrections.raw: Gaetjens v City of Loves Park
corrections.correct: Case caption is Sally Gaetjens v. Winnebago County, Illinois, 4 F.4th 487 (7th Cir. 2021). 'Loves Park' is the city where the searched home sat (in Winnebago County, IL); it is not the case name. Verified on CourtListener.

corrections.raw: Gaetjens 'Actual prongs?' (note left the test incomplete)
corrections.correct: Emergency-aid exigency: (1) an objectively reasonable basis to believe a person inside needs immediate aid (or that the premises pose an immediate safety threat); (2) a nexus between the emergency and the area entered; (3) scope limited to addressing the emergency. Verified against the 7th Cir. opinion (4 F.4th at 493-94).

corrections.raw: Us v Holiday (child abused in car, no car at house, officers went in, saw evidence in plain view, evidence suppressed)
corrections.correct: FLAG - UNVERIFIABLE this pass. 'United States v. Holiday' returns many cases on CourtListener and none was positively matched to these facts (child abuse in a car, no car at the house, warrantless entry anyway, plain-view evidence suppressed). Per the brief, NOT asserted. Omitted from the page body. The PRINCIPLE it illustrates (exceeding the scope/nexus of an exigency leads to suppression) is instead anchored to verified federal authority (Buie's scope limits; Gaetjens' nexus requirement). Verifier: attempt a precise match before any inclusion.

corrections.raw: State v McCall - protective sweeps as a matter of routine (patently unconstitutional)
corrections.correct: FLAG - NOT independently verified this pass (rate-limit cap reached). Multiple 'State v. McCall' decisions exist; reporter/year/jurisdiction not pinned down. Presented only as an unconfirmed state ILLUSTRATION paired with the controlling federal rule (Maryland v. Buie). The substantive point (routine/automatic sweeps are unconstitutional) IS the holding of Buie itself (494 U.S. at 336: 'decidedly not automati[c]'), which carries the page. Verifier: identify state + year or drop the illustration.

corrections.raw: State v Smith (Idaho, 2021) protective sweep - reasonable fear someone might be present that could harm officers
corrections.correct: FLAG - NOT independently verified this pass (rate-limit cap reached). An Idaho 2021 'State v. Smith' on protective sweeps was not confirmed to reporter/year on CourtListener. Presented only as an unconfirmed state ILLUSTRATION paired with Buie (the upheld-on-articulable-fear scenario tracks Buie's standard). Verifier: confirm the Idaho cite or drop the illustration.
